skills.name: update-skills-doc
skills.description: Reconcile this project's config.json/data.json skills catalog against the skills that actually exist on disk across every category's configured scan locations, then edit data.json (and config.json when a whole category is missing) to fix drift — skills missing from the catalog, entries no longer on disk, stale status/PR badges, and description drift — surfacing one grouped review so you approve each change (except auto-adding a freshly built skill). Use whenever the user asks to update the skill catalog, sync data.json, regenerate/refresh the skills reference, or check whether it's out of date, and as the final registration step after another skill-authoring workflow builds a new skill — even if the user doesn't name data.json explicitly.
skills.note: Ships a references/doc-data-model.md (canonical-name/shared-skill rules, deep-field guidance, review template). Included as a real, working example of what a fully-fleshed-out catalog entry looks like — replace it with your own skills, or keep it around to maintain this catalog.
skills.summary: Keeps this project's data.json skills catalog in sync with what's actually on disk by diffing and patching its data in place.
skills.descriptionIntro: Here's what reconciling the catalog against disk involves:
descriptionBullets: Reads config.json's categories and scans each category's declared scanPaths for SKILL.md files, resolving each to a canonical name, description, and invocation mode
Diffs disk against data.json's skills into four buckets: new skills to add, catalog-only entries to flag, stale badge/status drift, and description drift
Runs a lightweight PR/branch check (via a git-hosting MCP tool or the gh CLI) to turn merge-dependent badges into concrete recommendations
Surfaces one grouped review for approval — never deletes or flips a badge automatically, except auto-adding a skill that was just built by another skill-authoring workflow
Edits data.json's skills array directly (and config.json only when adding a whole missing category); never touches src/ or scripts/
howToUse: Triggered by asking to update/sync/refresh the skill catalog, or automatically as the final step after another skill-authoring workflow builds a new skill.
Walks every scan location declared in config.json's categories — whatever they are for this project — reading each SKILL.md's frontmatter, and the full body for any skill it's about to add.
Parses data.json's current skills and diffs them against what it found on disk.
For any item involving merge state (e.g. a "Draft PR #123" badge), checks a git-hosting MCP tool or gh pr view and turns that into a concrete recommendation.

Presents one grouped review of every add/removal/badge-drift/description-drift item and pauses, waiting for your per-item approve or skip before changing anything — except a just-built skill's own entry, which it auto-adds.
Once you respond, applies only the items you approved, leaves skipped ones untouched, validates data.json against config.json, and runs npm run build to confirm the catalog still builds cleanly.

skills.name: writing-great-skills
skills.description: Reference for writing and editing skills well — the vocabulary and principles that make a skill predictable.
skills.summary: Reference for the vocabulary and principles that make a skill predictable, covering invocation, descriptions, hierarchy, and pruning.
skills.descriptionIntro: The body covers a set of core principles that make a skill predictable, spanning several topics.
descriptionBullets: Invocation: choosing between model-invoked (autonomous, costs context load) and user-invoked (manual, costs cognitive load) skills, plus when to add a router skill
Writing the description: front-loading the leading word, one trigger per branch, cutting identity already covered in the body
Information hierarchy: the ladder from in-skill steps to in-skill reference to external/disclosed reference, and how completion criteria drive thorough legwork
When to split: cutting skills by invocation (a distinct leading word) or by sequence (to avoid premature completion)
Pruning: keeping a single source of truth, checking relevance, and aggressively hunting no-op sentences
Leading words: reusing pretrained concepts to anchor execution and invocation in fewer tokens
Failure modes: diagnosing premature completion, duplication, sediment, sprawl, no-ops, and negation
howToUse: Consult the relevant section on demand — invocation choice, description writing, information hierarchy, splitting, pruning, leading words, or failure diagnosis — when authoring or editing a skill.

skills.name: onboard-feature
skills.description: Explores an unfamiliar feature or area of the codebase end-to-end — every entry point, the full data/control flow, key files and functions, tests, and gotchas — then delivers a structured walkthrough so the developer has enough context to work on it independently. Use whenever the user wants to understand, explain, walk through, ramp up on, get up to speed on, or onboard to a feature, module, subsystem, or flow they haven't worked on before — including casual or indirect phrasing like "how does X work", "walk me through the Y flow", "I need to fix a bug in Z but don't know where to start", or just naming a feature, ticket, or file without asking for an explanation outright.
skills.summary: Explores an unfamiliar feature end-to-end — entry points, full trace, tests, gotchas — into a structured onboarding walkthrough.
howToUse: Restates the feature or flow in one sentence, asking a clarifying question only if the request is genuinely ambiguous.
Identifies every entry point into the feature (UI, API, CLI, jobs, webhooks) rather than just the one the user mentioned.
Traces the code through every layer from trigger to persistence and back, following it across repo boundaries and collecting types, tests, config, and history along the way.
Confirms it can answer key questions about the most important files, the happy path, non-obvious surprises, and where to start working, before writing anything.
Presents the findings in a structured markdown template ordered by execution flow, citing real file paths and functions.
Offers to go deeper into any file, branch, or adjacent feature that came up during exploration.

skills.name: skill-creator
skills.description: Create new skills, modify and improve existing skills, and measure skill performance. Use when users want to create a skill from scratch, edit, or optimize an existing skill, run evals to test a skill, benchmark skill performance with variance analysis, or optimize a skill's description for better triggering accuracy.
skills.summary: Guides drafting and iteratively improving Agent Skills via with-skill/baseline evals, a review viewer, and description optimization.
skills.descriptionIntro: At a high level, the skill frames creating and improving a skill as one continuous loop with these stages.
descriptionBullets: Deciding what the skill should do and drafting it
Writing test prompts and running them with and without the skill via subagents
Helping the user evaluate results qualitatively (via a generated review viewer) and quantitatively (via drafted assertions and benchmarks)
Rewriting the skill based on feedback and repeating the loop until satisfied
Expanding the test set and trying again at larger scale
Optionally optimizing the skill's description for better triggering accuracy using a train/test eval loop
Packaging the finished skill into an installable .skill file when supported
howToUse: Captures intent and interviews the user about triggers, output format, and whether test cases are needed.
Writes or edits the SKILL.md following progressive disclosure and skill-writing patterns.
Drafts 2-3 test prompts, shares them for approval, then spawns parallel with-skill and baseline subagent runs.
While runs are in progress, drafts quantitative assertions and explains them to the user.
Grades each run's outputs, aggregates them into a benchmark, and launches the eval-viewer for qualitative and quantitative review, then pauses for the user to review and give feedback.
Reads the user's feedback and improves the skill, generalizing from it rather than overfitting to the examples.
Reruns test cases in a new iteration, repeating the review loop until the user is happy, feedback is all empty, or progress stalls.
Optionally runs the description-optimization loop — generating trigger eval queries, getting user review, running the optimization script, and applying the best description.
Optionally packages the finished skill into a .skill file for the user.

skills.name: quick-research
skills.description: Runs a fast, scoped web-research pass on a topic and returns a short list of sourced findings, each paired with a concrete suggestion for how it could change a plan, design, or skill under construction. Use when the user (or another skill) wants a quick best-practices check or a sanity check against current knowledge before committing to a design — e.g. "check what's out there for X first", "any best practices I should know about?", or as a pre-step another skill runs before interviewing the user. Not for exhaustive, report-length deep dives — for that, the user should ask for a full research report instead.
skills.summary: Runs a fast, scoped web-research pass and returns sourced findings paired with concrete suggested changes to a plan.
howToUse: Scopes the research in one line — what's being investigated and what decision it will inform — asking if unclear or using the caller's given scope.
Decomposes the topic into 2-4 tight sub-questions, flagging if the ask has grown beyond a quick check.
Searches each sub-question with 2-3 queries, stopping once answered or at diminishing returns.
Reads past search snippets, fetching the full page for the 1-3 sources that actually matter.
Tracks sourcing and recency for every claim, flagging single-source or stale claims as lower-confidence.
Makes sure it can explain any unfamiliar concept in plain language before surfacing it, reading further if it can't.

Synthesizes findings into a structured template and presents them as proposals — to the user directly, or back to a calling skill — rather than a long-form report, then pauses for approval before anything is treated as adopted.

skills.name: super-skill-builder
skills.description: Builds a new Agent Skill by convening a panel of installed skill-builder skills, running each one in an isolated subagent against the same brief, then comparing the candidates and synthesizing the strongest single skill. Use this whenever the user wants to create, build, author, generate, or "turn something into" a skill — including phrasings like "make a skill for X", "build a new skill", "I want a skill that…", or "can you turn this workflow into a skill" — even if they don't name this skill explicitly.
skills.summary: Orchestrates a panel of skill-builder skills to each draft a candidate skill, then compares and synthesizes the best one.
howToUse: Captures the user's raw skill request verbatim as the brief and restates the skill's rough intent without designing yet.
Assembles the panel of skill-builder skills, safety-checking and installing any missing ones with user approval, then bridges them into the host agent's own skill directory.
Reads each panelist's SKILL.md to build a deduped set of grill questions covering every panelist's decision points.
Offers an optional pre-grill web research pass on best practices, presenting findings for the user to accept or decline before the grill starts.
Runs a one-question-at-a-time grill session over the question set and turns the answers into a consolidated spec.
Fans out the verbatim brief and spec to one isolated subagent per panelist, each building its own candidate skill at a unique path.
Compares the candidates with a table and pros/cons, proposes a synthesis plan, and pauses for the user to confirm or adjust it.
Writes the synthesized skill to the host agent's skill directory per the confirmed plan.
Verifies the finished skill against both the consolidated spec and the original verbatim brief, fixing any gaps found.
Removes the throwaway candidate artifacts with user approval, or lists them for manual deletion if removal is blocked.
Registers the new skill in the personal skills catalog via update-skills-doc, if that skill is installed.

skills.name: enhanced-babysit
skills.description: Keep a GitHub pull request merge-ready by resolving conflicts, triaging comments (including Bugbot), and fixing CI in a loop, using the GitKraken/GitLens MCP for PR details, comments, and reviews instead of the gh CLI wherever that MCP has a tool for it. A personal, GitKraken-powered evolution of the built-in "babysit" skill. Use when the user asks to babysit a PR, keep a PR merge-ready, or triage PR comments/CI.
skills.summary: Keeps a GitHub PR merge-ready by looping conflict resolution, comment triage, and CI fixes via the GitKraken MCP.
skills.descriptionIntro: It's upfront about a few gaps where the GitKraken MCP has no equivalent tool and falls back to gh/shell instead.
descriptionBullets: No CI/checks status tool, so CI diagnosis always falls back to gh
No local working-tree status tool, so uncommitted-change checks use git status/diff via shell
No thread-resolve or inline-reply tool, so it can only post a general PR comment or a new review rather than resolving an individual thread
howToUse: Identifies the PR by resolving the repo org/name from the git remote and the PR number via gh, then uses GitKraken tools exclusively afterward.
Resolves merge conflicts by fetching and rebasing/merging against the base branch, editing conflicted files directly, and stopping to ask if the two sides' intent genuinely conflicts.
Triages unresolved PR comments, validating each one (especially Bugbot) before fixing valid issues or explaining disagreement, then posts a summary comment referencing the fix and commit.

Diagnoses and fixes CI failures caused by this PR's own changes via gh, merging in the latest base branch if failures look unrelated, and repeats the conflict/comment/CI loop until the PR is merge-ready.

skills.name: regression-tracer
skills.description: Traces a suspected code regression back through git history to recover what the code looked like before it broke, then adapts that prior implementation into a fix instead of inventing one from scratch. Use when code looks dead, unused, or orphaned (e.g. a CSS class, prop, or config key referenced but never defined); when behavior, styling, or structure is inconsistent across otherwise-parallel/sibling implementations (one component/handler/branch got a real port, another didn't); when the user reports something "used to work", "broke recently", or "looks like a leftover from a refactor"; or when a bug's root cause seems to be something a past change removed, forgot to port, or renamed to nothing rather than a gap in the original design. Trigger this proactively even when the user doesn't say "regression" or mention git by name — noticing "there's no CSS rule for this class" or "why does this one branch differ from its three siblings" is the signal itself, before writing a fix from scratch.
skills.summary: Traces a suspected regression through git history, verifies it via a sibling check, then adapts the recovered fix forward.
skills.descriptionIntro: A trace only counts as complete once every one of these holds.
descriptionBullets: A specific commit is cited by sha and message, not a vague guess
The old and new code are actually shown, not just described
The verdict (regression, intentional, or inconclusive) was stated outright
The fix is ported forward from the recovered implementation, not a blind revert or a from-scratch reinvention
The user saw the evidence before the fix landed
If a bisect was used, the culprit commit is named and the working tree is confirmed back to normal
howToUse: Picks a branch — a known suspect to diff directly, or an unknown/behavioral case that needs bisecting to localize first — and names which branch it's in.
Recovers the exact pre-regression code and the commit(s) that touched it, using the git commands for the chosen branch.
Runs a sibling check, diffing every comparable element from the same commit to see if only the suspect was dropped or renamed.
States an explicit verdict — regression, intentional, or no regression found/inconclusive — based on the sibling check evidence.
Walks the user through the trigger, the commit, the before/after code, and the verdict in plain conversation before proposing any fix, then pauses for the user's reaction.
Adapts the recovered implementation forward through any conventions that have since changed, rather than pasting it back verbatim or reinventing it from scratch.

skills.name: update-skills-doc
skills.description: Reconcile the personal Swagger-style skills catalog at ~/.cursor/skills/skills-reference.html against the skills that actually exist on disk across every skill folder, then surgically edit its CATEGORIES data in place to fix drift — skills missing from the doc, entries no longer on disk, stale status/PR badges, and description drift — surfacing one grouped review so you approve each change (except auto-adding a freshly built skill). Use whenever the user asks to update the skill doc, sync the skills reference, regenerate/refresh the skills catalog, or check whether it's out of date, and as the final registration step of super-skill-builder after a new skill is created — even if the user doesn't name the HTML file explicitly.
skills.note: A different tool than this project's own update-skills-doc (categoryId: project) — this one maintains a separate HTML catalog at ~/.cursor/skills/skills-reference.html, not this repo's config.json/data.json.
skills.summary: Reconciles a personal skills-reference.html catalog against disk, editing its CATEGORIES data in place after one grouped approval.
skills.descriptionIntro: The skill enforces a few hard guardrails around how it edits the catalog.
descriptionBullets: Only one automatic change is ever applied without asking: the just-built skill's add during super-skill-builder registration mode
It never deletes a doc entry automatically — doc-only entries are surfaced, not removed
It never flips a badge, status, or description without approval, even when a merge check says it's safe
It edits the CATEGORIES data in place and never regenerates or touches the render()/search logic
It never invokes super-skill-builder itself, staying strictly the callee
howToUse: Fixes the mode — standalone (user-triggered) or super-skill-builder registration (a just-built skill was passed in) — and records that skill's name, path, and category if in registration mode.
Enumerates every skill on disk across the scan map, resolving each to its canonical name, description, and invocation.
Parses the doc's existing CATEGORIES entries and flags any scan-map category missing from the doc.
Diffs disk against the doc into four drift buckets: additions, doc-only entries, badge/status drift, and description drift.
Runs a lightweight branch/PR check to turn merge-dependent badge or doc-only questions into concrete recommendations.
Presents one grouped review of every drift item for approval, auto-applying only the just-built skill's addition in registration mode, then pauses for the user's per-item approvals.
Applies the approved edits directly in the CATEGORIES data, confirms the file still parses and renders, and summarizes what was applied, skipped, or deferred.

skills.name: grill-me
skills.description: A relentless interview to sharpen a plan or design.
skills.summary: Manual trigger that runs a /grilling session to relentlessly interview the user about their plan.
howToUse: Runs a /grilling session to relentlessly interview the user about their plan or design.

skills.name: grilling
skills.description: Grill the user relentlessly about a plan or design. Use when the user wants to stress-test a plan before building, or uses any 'grill' trigger phrases.
skills.summary: Interviews the user one question at a time about every branch of a plan, recommending an answer each time.
howToUse: Interviews the user one question at a time about each branch of the plan, recommending an answer for each, looking up discoverable facts itself rather than asking, and holds off on enacting the plan until the user confirms shared understanding.

skills.name: morning-triage
skills.description: Generates a personal daily digest canvas of pull requests and Linear issues that need attention, combining GitLens Launchpad prioritization across the frontend and on-prem repos with Linear issues assigned to the user. Use when the user asks for their morning triage, daily digest, "what needs my attention", a PR/issue triage, or a review queue.
skills.summary: Builds a personal canvas digest of PRs (via GitLens Launchpad) and in-progress Linear issues assigned to the user.
howToUse: Calls GitLens Launchpad once per repo (frontend and on-prem) to gather PRs already grouped by what needs attention, merging and deduping results across both.
Calls the Linear list_issues tool for issues assigned to the user, filtering to only started/unstarted issues and sorting by priority then recency.
Skips any data source that errors or returns nothing, mentioning the gap in the chat reply rather than showing an error or empty section in the canvas.
Writes (or overwrites) a canvas file with a header stat summary, one section per non-empty Launchpad category, and a Linear issues section grouped by priority, omitting any section with zero items.
Replies in chat with a 1-2 sentence summary of the highest-priority items and a link to the canvas.

skills.name: post-on-prem-issue
skills.description: Hand off a confirmed backend/on-prem bug to the backend team — safely sync the local on-prem repo, reconfirm the issue still reproduces, and only then draft and post a succinct Linear issue to the Product (SPR) team after your explicit approval.
skills.summary: Hands a confirmed backend bug to the Product/SPR Linear team, gated by sync, reproduction, and explicit approval.
skills.descriptionIntro: The workflow leans on a few guiding values to keep the handoff clean and safe.
descriptionBullets: Confirms before filing, since a local checkout can lag develop and the most common waste is filing an already-fixed bug
Keeps issues succinct — just what's needed to reproduce and understand the problem
Never posts without explicit approval; the full metadata and body are reviewed in chat first
Never touches the user's git work beyond a conservative sync, stopping and reporting instead of mutating a risky tree
howToUse: Offers to sync the on-prem repo to the latest develop, doing a safe checkout-and-pull only on a clean tree and otherwise pasting git status and handing the decision back to the user, then pauses for the user's answer.
Asks the user to reconfirm the issue still reproduces against the now-current code, stopping and filing nothing if it's already resolved.
Asks which issue template to use — Bug, Suggestion, or custom — and loads that template's defaults.
Drafts the title and body itself following the template, then prompts the user for status, assignee, project, and labels using live options pulled from Linear.
Presents the full drafted issue for review and waits for explicit approval before calling save_issue to post it, then returns the created issue's identifier and URL.

skills.name: create-subagent
skills.description: Create custom subagents for specialized AI tasks. Use when you want to create a new type of subagent, set up task-specific agents, configure code reviewers, debuggers, or domain-specific assistants with custom prompts.
skills.summary: Guides creating custom Cursor subagents: file format, effective descriptions, and project vs. user-level placement.
skills.descriptionIntro: Explains what subagents give you when used well:
descriptionBullets: Preserve context by isolating exploration from the main conversation
Specialize behavior with focused system prompts for specific domains
Reuse configurations across projects with user-level subagents
howToUse: Decide whether the subagent should be project-level (shared, in .cursor/agents/) or user-level (personal, in ~/.cursor/agents/).
Create the markdown file in the chosen agents directory.
Write the frontmatter with the required name and description fields.
Write the body as the subagent's system prompt, covering its workflow, output format, and constraints.
Test the new subagent by asking the AI to use it for a task.

skills.name: migrate-to-skills
skills.description: Convert 'Applied intelligently' Cursor rules (.cursor/rules/*.mdc) and slash commands (.cursor/commands/*.md) to Agent Skills format (.cursor/skills/). Use when you want to migrate rules or commands to skills, convert .mdc rules to SKILL.md format, or consolidate commands into the skills directory.
skills.summary: Converts eligible 'Applied intelligently' rules and slash commands into Agent Skills format, preserving body content verbatim.
howToUse: Creates the project and/or user skills directories if they don't already exist.
Dispatches parallel subagents to find eligible rule and command files (rules must have a description but no globs and no alwaysApply: true; commands are always eligible).
For each eligible file, reads it and writes a new SKILL.md with the required frontmatter while copying the original body content verbatim.
Deletes the original rule or command file after migrating it.
Summarizes the migrated files to the user, noting that the migration can be undone if they ask.

skills.name: shell
skills.description: Runs the rest of a /shell request as a literal shell command. Use only when the user explicitly invokes /shell and wants the following text executed directly in the terminal.
skills.summary: Executes the text after a /shell invocation directly as a terminal command, without rewriting or inspecting it first.
howToUse: Treats all text after /shell as the literal command, runs it immediately without rewriting, explaining, or inspecting the repo first, then briefly reports the exit status and key stdout/stderr.

skills.name: statusline
skills.description: Configure a custom status line in the CLI. Use when the user mentions status line, statusline, statusLine, CLI status bar, prompt footer customization, or wants to add session context above the prompt.
skills.summary: Documents how to configure and script a custom CLI status line via cli-config.json and its JSON stdin session payload.
howToUse: Adds a statusLine command entry to ~/.cursor/cli-config.json that spawns a script or inline command receiving a JSON session payload on stdin and prints text (optionally ANSI-colored, multi-line) rendered above the prompt.

skills.name: onboard
skills.description: Use /onboard for a focused Cursor onboarding flow that learns basic preferences, picks a first goal, and routes the user to the right next action.
skills.summary: Runs a scripted /onboard interview that learns the user's name/context, saves it to a personal rule, then routes to a next step.
skills.descriptionIntro: Follows a strict set of hard rules to keep onboarding lightweight and non-intrusive:
descriptionBullets: Asks one question at a time and only uses tools within the defined Tool Boundary
Never inspects files, browses MCP descriptors, or changes settings during onboarding
Answers normal Cursor questions directly, then asks whether to continue or stop onboarding
Stops the flow once the user seems done, and drops the Tool Boundary restrictions afterward
howToUse: Asks the user's preferred name, then pauses for their answer.
Asks about their work context and typical projects, then pauses for their answer.
Saves the name and work context to a personal 'User onboarding preferences' rule (skipped if the user declined or memory saving is unavailable).
Asks the user to pick a first goal — getting set up, starting a new project, automating their job, working on an existing project, or something else — then pauses for their choice.
Routes through a short guided flow for the chosen goal, asking one diagnostic question at a time and pausing for each answer.
Produces a compact handoff (recommended next step, suggested prompt, mode/tool to use) and, when Plan mode applies, asks whether to switch, pausing for the user's confirmation before doing so.

skills.name: canvas
skills.description: A Cursor Canvas is a live React app that the user can open beside the chat. You MUST use a canvas when the agent produces a standalone analytical artifact — quantitative analyses, billing investigations, security audits, architecture reviews, data-heavy content, timelines, charts, tables, interactive explorations, repeatable tools, or any response that benefits from visual layout. Especially prefer a canvas when presenting results from MCP tools (Datadog, Databricks, Linear, Sentry, Slack, etc.) where the data is the deliverable — render it in a rich canvas rather than dumping it into a markdown table or code block. If you catch yourself about to write a markdown table, stop and use a canvas instead. You MUST also read this skill whenever you create, edit, or debug any .canvas.tsx file.
skills.summary: Defines when and how to build a standalone Cursor Canvas (.canvas.tsx), including file rules, design guardrails, and a slop self-check.
skills.descriptionIntro: Reserves a canvas for genuine standalone artifacts, using it when the agent produces:
descriptionBullets: Quantitative analyses and metrics breakdowns
Billing or account investigations with structured findings from database queries
Security audits or architecture reviews with categorized findings
Cross-system data analyses and overlap reports
Structured data from MCP tools where the data itself is the deliverable
Financial analyses, margin decompositions, and usage trend reports, or large tables the user asked to see
howToUse: Decides whether the requested output truly warrants its own standalone artifact rather than a normal chat reply.
Writes a single self-contained .canvas.tsx file at the exact managed canvases/ path, importing only from cursor/canvas, embedding all data inline with no empty placeholder sections.
Applies design guardrails (visual hierarchy, theme tokens, no gradients/emojis/shadows) and runs a pre-delivery self-check before returning the canvas.
Introduces the canvas to the user with a markdown link to its absolute path, adding a short explanation for a first or unsolicited canvas.
Troubleshoots a blank canvas by confirming it was saved to the exact managed directory path rather than debugging the directory itself.

skills.name: create-skill
skills.description: Create Cursor Agent Skills. Use when authoring a new skill or asking about SKILL.md structure.
skills.summary: Teaches how to author effective Cursor Agent Skills: SKILL.md structure, description writing, conciseness, and a 4-phase workflow.
skills.descriptionIntro: Lays out core principles for writing effective skills:
descriptionBullets: Keep content concise since the context window is shared with conversation history and other skills
Keep SKILL.md under 500 lines for optimal performance
Use progressive disclosure, putting essentials in SKILL.md and details in separate reference files
Match the degree of freedom (text instructions, templates, or exact scripts) to how fragile the task is
howToUse: Gathers requirements in a discovery phase — the skill's purpose, storage location, trigger scenarios, and any constraints or existing patterns — pausing for the user's answers as needed.
Designs the skill: drafts the name, writes a specific third-person description with trigger terms, and outlines needed sections and supporting files.
Implements the skill by creating the directory structure, writing SKILL.md with frontmatter, and adding any reference files or utility scripts.
Verifies the result against a checklist — length, description quality, consistent terminology, one-level-deep references, and discoverability.

skills.name: create-rule
skills.description: Create Cursor rules for persistent AI guidance. Use when you want to create a rule, add coding standards, set up project conventions, configure file-specific patterns, create RULE.md files, or asks about .cursor/rules/ or AGENTS.md.
skills.summary: Guides creating .mdc rules in .cursor/rules/, covering frontmatter scope/globs and keeping rules short and concrete.
skills.descriptionIntro: Recommends keeping rules concise, following a few best practices:
descriptionBullets: Keep rules under 50 lines
Cover one concern per rule, splitting large rules into focused pieces
Write actionable guidance like clear internal docs
Include concrete examples of how to fix issues where possible
howToUse: Gathers requirements on the rule's purpose, whether it should always apply or target specific files, and the glob patterns, pausing to ask the user when scope or patterns are unclear.
Creates a .mdc file in .cursor/rules/ with frontmatter (description, globs, alwaysApply) matching the chosen scope.
Writes the rule body concisely, under 50 lines, with concrete examples of the standard being enforced.

skills.name: automate
skills.description: Use this skill to create Cursor Automations.
skills.summary: Interactively drafts a new Cursor Automation (trigger, tools, prompt) under strict integration/MCP gates, then hands off to the editor.
skills.descriptionIntro: Follows a strict set of house rules while drafting an automation:
descriptionBullets: Uses plain language only in chat, never showing MCP/tool/proto names or raw payloads unless asked
Builds and validates the YAML internally, only revealing it if the user explicitly asks
Never submits, opens a URL, or falls back automatically — the only finish path is a reviewed draft the user approves
Only prepares new automations; it does not list, inspect, or update existing ones from chat
Only references a repo file inline when the automation runs in that same repo and the file is actually committed there
howToUse: Checks whether the in-app Automations editor handoff is available, stopping immediately if it isn't.
Captures the user's intent (trigger, action, outcome) and proactively runs integration discovery, sending one short question and pausing for the answer only if key details are still missing.
Works through completeness gates for trigger, tools, prompt, naming, and any Slack/Git/MCP-specific requirements.
Asks one consolidated question for anything integration discovery couldn't resolve, then pauses for the user's answer.
Shows a compact draft table for approval, then pauses; once approved and the user confirms they're ready, opens the Automations editor with the reviewed draft.

skills.name: sdk
skills.description: Guide users building apps, scripts, CI pipelines, or automations on top of the Cursor SDK - TypeScript (`@cursor/sdk`) or Python (`cursor-sdk` / `cursor_sdk`). Use when the user mentions integrating, installing, or writing code against the Cursor SDK; says `Agent.create`, `Agent.prompt`, `Agent.resume`, `agent.send`, `run.stream`, `run.messages`, `CursorAgentError`, `@cursor/sdk`, `cursor-sdk`, or `cursor_sdk`; asks to run Cursor agents programmatically from a script, CI/CD pipeline, GitHub Action, backend service, or other code outside the Cursor IDE; wants to pick between local and cloud runtime, configure MCP servers for an SDK agent, or handle streaming, cancellation, or errors; or is wiring Cursor into an automation, bot, or REST `/v1/agents` migration. Use eagerly rather than answering from memory; the SDK surface evolves and this skill is the source of truth for the external packages.
skills.summary: Guides building integrations on the Cursor SDK (TypeScript/Python): picking a language and invocation pattern, and avoiding five common pitfalls.
skills.descriptionIntro: Calls out five traps that catch almost every new SDK integration:
descriptionBullets: Forgetting to explicitly set local vs cloud runtime, which silently defaults to local
Conflating a thrown CursorAgentError (run never executed) with a returned error status (run executed and failed)
Skipping disposal of the agent, which leaks child processes, databases, and memory
Skipping wait() even when not streaming, leaving you unable to tell if a run finished, errored, or was cancelled
Assuming every run operation (like cancel) is supported on every runtime instead of guarding with supports()
howToUse: Determines which language variant (TypeScript or Python) to use based on what the user named, codebase signals, or by asking directly.
Picks the invocation pattern that fits the job: one-shot Agent.prompt, Agent.create plus agent.send for durable multi-turn/streaming use, or Agent.resume to continue an existing agent later.
Applies the fixes for the five common traps and the production best practices for unattended integrations.

Summarizes the findings in a severity-sorted markdown table (or reports no diff/no issues), without fixing anything unless asked.

If reviewing a specific PR or branch, checks that target out first, asking before stashing any local changes and pausing for confirmation if needed.

skills.name: split-to-prs
skills.description: Split current work into small reviewable PRs. Use when the user asks to split a chat, set of changes, branch, or PR.
skills.summary: Plans and, after approval, executes splitting a pile of work into several small, reviewer-aligned PRs without destructive git ops.
skills.descriptionIntro: Follows hard rules to avoid losing or misrepresenting work while splitting:
descriptionBullets: Never creates branches, commits, pushes, or opens PRs until the split plan is approved
Never discards user work or runs destructive git commands without explicit approval
Always saves a recoverable snapshot before moving any work around
Stages only named files or hunks, never a blanket git add
howToUse: Compares the current work (committed and uncommitted) against the default branch and summarizes the natural slices, using ownership files to find reviewer boundaries.

Once approved, saves a recoverable snapshot, then creates a branch per slice, staging and committing only the planned files or hunks, and opens each PR.
Reports back the PR titles and URLs plus anything left on the original branch or working tree.

skills.name: loop

For no interval (dynamic mode), runs the prompt now, then self-paces by arming a time-based or event-based wake for the next run, re-arming after each wake.
Confirms the schedule and cadence to the user, and stops the loop when asked.

skills.name: test-driven-development
skills.description: Use when implementing any feature or bugfix, before writing implementation code
skills.summary: Enforces strict red-green-refactor TDD: write a failing test, watch it fail correctly, write minimal code to pass, then refactor.
howToUse: Write one minimal failing test that names and demonstrates the desired behavior
Run the test and confirm it fails for the expected reason, not from a typo or setup error
Write the simplest implementation code that makes the test pass, without adding extra features
Run the tests again and confirm the new test and all existing tests pass with clean output
Refactor the code to remove duplication and improve clarity while keeping tests green
Repeat the cycle for the next behavior, and write a failing test to reproduce any bug before fixing it

skills.name: systematic-debugging
skills.description: Use when encountering any bug, test failure, or unexpected behavior, before proposing fixes
skills.summary: Forces root-cause investigation through four ordered phases before any bug fix may be attempted.
skills.descriptionIntro: The skill defines four mandatory phases that must be completed in order before any fix is proposed.
descriptionBullets: Phase 1 Root Cause Investigation: read error messages fully, reproduce the issue consistently, check recent changes, gather diagnostic evidence across component boundaries in multi-component systems, and trace data flow backward to its source
Phase 2 Pattern Analysis: find working examples similar to the broken code, compare against reference implementations, identify every difference, and understand dependencies
Phase 3 Hypothesis and Testing: form a single specific hypothesis, test it with the smallest possible change, and verify before continuing or forming a new hypothesis

Phase 4 Implementation: create a failing test case, implement a single fix for the root cause, verify it, and if three or more fixes fail, stop and question the underlying architecture instead of continuing to patch
howToUse: Read error messages and stack traces completely rather than skipping past them
Reproduce the issue consistently and check recent changes such as git diff, new dependencies, or config that could have caused it
For multi-component systems, add diagnostic instrumentation at each boundary to find where the failure actually occurs
Trace data flow backward from the error to find its origin rather than fixing at the symptom
Find working examples in the codebase and compare them against the broken code to identify every difference
Form a single, specific hypothesis about the root cause and test it with the smallest possible change
If the hypothesis doesn't hold, form a new one rather than layering more fixes on top
Create a failing test that reproduces the bug before implementing any fix
Implement one fix addressing the root cause, then verify it resolves the issue without breaking other tests
If three or more fixes have failed, stop and discuss whether the underlying architecture is wrong instead of attempting another fix

Documents how skills are accessed across platforms: the Skill tool in Claude Code, the skill tool in Copilot CLI, and activate_skill in Gemini CLI

Dispatch all the agents in parallel rather than one at a time

skills.name: finishing-a-development-branch

Presents exactly four completion options: merge locally, push and create a pull request, keep the branch as-is, or discard the work

Cleans up the git worktree afterward for the merge and discard options, but preserves it for the keep-as-is and PR options

Determine the base branch the feature branch diverged from
Present exactly four options to the user (merge locally, create a PR, keep as-is, discard), then pauses for their choice
Execute the chosen option: merge and delete the branch, push and open a PR, leave the branch alone, or delete the branch after requiring a typed 'discard' confirmation, pausing for that confirmation
Remove the git worktree afterward if the option was merge or discard, keeping it for the other two options

If upcoming questions will be visual, offer a browser-based visual companion as its own standalone message, then pauses for the user's response
Ask clarifying questions one at a time, preferring multiple choice, then pauses for the user's answer each time

Present the design in scaled sections, pausing for approval after each section
Write the approved design to a dated spec file under docs/superpowers/specs and commit it
Self-review the spec for placeholders, contradictions, ambiguity, and scope, fixing issues inline

skills.name: writing-plans

Breaks work into bite-sized 2-5 minute steps (write test, verify fail, implement, verify pass, commit) following DRY, YAGNI, and TDD
Requires a standard plan header naming the required execution sub-skill, goal, architecture, and tech stack
Forbids placeholders like 'TBD', 'add appropriate error handling', or references to undefined types or functions
Self-reviews the finished plan against the spec for coverage gaps, placeholders, and type/naming consistency before finalizing
Offers the user a choice between subagent-driven or inline plan execution after saving
howToUse: Check whether the spec covers multiple independent subsystems and suggest splitting into separate plans if so
Map out which files will be created or modified and what each one is responsible for before defining tasks
Write the plan with the required header, then break each task into bite-sized steps with exact file paths, complete code, and exact commands
Avoid any placeholders, vague instructions, or undefined references, ensuring every step is fully spelled out
Self-review the finished plan against the spec for coverage, placeholders, and type consistency, fixing issues inline
Save the plan to docs/superpowers/plans and offer the user a choice between subagent-driven or inline execution, then pauses for their choice

skills.name: requesting-code-review

howToUse: Get the base and head git SHAs for the change being reviewed
Dispatch a code-reviewer subagent using the template, filling in what was implemented, the requirements, and the SHAs
Fix Critical issues immediately, fix Important issues before proceeding, note Minor issues for later, and push back with technical reasoning if the reviewer seems wrong

skills.name: receiving-code-review
skills.description: Use when receiving code review feedback, before implementing suggestions, especially if feedback seems unclear or technically questionable - requires technical rigor and verification, not performative agreement or blind implementation
skills.summary: Requires verifying and technically evaluating code review feedback before implementing it, forbidding performative agreement and enabling reasoned pushback.
skills.descriptionIntro: The skill defines a six-step response pattern plus source-specific handling rules for incoming feedback.
descriptionBullets: Follows a fixed response pattern: read fully, restate the requirement, verify against the codebase, evaluate technical soundness, respond, then implement one item at a time
Forbids performative responses like 'You're absolutely right!' or implementing before verification
Requires stopping to ask for clarification on any unclear item rather than implementing only the clear ones
Applies different trust levels depending on source: trusted for the human partner, but skeptically verified for external reviewers across a checklist
Applies a YAGNI check when a reviewer suggests 'implementing properly' by grepping for actual usage first
Orders implementation of multi-item feedback as blocking issues, then simple fixes, then complex fixes, testing each individually
Requires technical reasoning rather than defensiveness when pushing back, and a factual correction with no apology when the pushback turns out wrong
Requires replying to inline GitHub review comments in their own thread rather than as a top-level comment
howToUse: Read the complete feedback without reacting
Restate the requirement in your own words or ask for clarification if anything is unclear, then pauses until all unclear items are resolved
Verify the feedback against the actual codebase before agreeing with it
Evaluate whether the suggestion is technically sound for this specific codebase
Respond with a technical acknowledgment or a reasoned pushback, never performative agreement or gratitude
Implement accepted items one at a time, in order of blocking issues then simple fixes then complex fixes, testing each individually

skills.name: writing-skills
skills.description: Use when creating new skills, editing existing skills, or verifying skills work before deployment
skills.summary: Applies TDD discipline to authoring SKILL.md files: run baseline pressure tests without the skill, write the minimal skill to fix the failures, then close loopholes.
skills.descriptionIntro: The skill maps the TDD cycle onto skill creation and lays out structure, discoverability, and testing requirements.
descriptionBullets: Maps TDD concepts onto skill creation: pressure scenarios are test cases, the SKILL.md is production code, baseline agent violations are RED, and compliance is GREEN
Defines skill types (technique, pattern, reference) and when to create a skill versus when not to

Specifies required SKILL.md structure: YAML frontmatter with name and description under 1024 characters, description starting with 'Use when...' describing only triggering conditions and never the workflow
Gives Claude Search Optimization guidance for descriptions, keyword coverage, naming, and token efficiency so skills are discoverable and cheap to load
Requires cross-referencing other skills by name with REQUIRED SUB-SKILL or REQUIRED BACKGROUND markers instead of force-loading @ links
Restricts flowcharts to non-obvious decision points, never for reference material or linear instructions
Requires testing every skill type (discipline-enforcing, technique, pattern, reference) with appropriate pressure or application scenarios before deployment
Provides a full RED-GREEN-REFACTOR checklist and anti-patterns (narrative examples, multi-language dilution, code in flowcharts, generic labels) to avoid
howToUse: Run pressure scenarios with a subagent without the skill present and document the baseline rationalizations and failures verbatim
Write a minimal SKILL.md addressing those specific baseline failures, following the required frontmatter and structure conventions
Re-run the same scenarios with the skill present and verify the agent now complies
Identify any new rationalizations the agent finds, add explicit counters and a red-flags list, and re-test until bulletproof
Commit the finished skill to git, fully testing and deploying each skill before starting the next one

skills.name: verification-before-completion
skills.description: Use when about to claim work is complete, fixed, or passing, before committing or creating PRs - requires running verification commands and confirming output before making any success claims; evidence before assertions always
skills.summary: Forbids any claim of completion, success, or passing tests without first running the actual verification command and reading its full output.
skills.descriptionIntro: The skill defines a gate function that must run before any completion or success claim.
descriptionBullets: Requires identifying the exact command that proves a claim, running it fresh and in full, reading the complete output, and only then making the claim
Lists common claims (tests pass, linter clean, build succeeds, bug fixed, regression test works, agent completed, requirements met) with what evidence each actually requires
Flags wording like 'should', 'probably', or expressions of satisfaction made before verification as red flags to stop on
Requires independently verifying subagent or delegated-agent success reports via the actual version-control diff rather than trusting the report
Requires a red-green cycle (revert the fix, confirm the test now fails, restore the fix) to prove a regression test actually catches the bug
howToUse: Identify which command actually proves the claim being made
Run that full command fresh, not relying on a previous run or partial check
Read the complete output, checking the exit code and counting failures
Only state the claim once the output confirms it, citing the evidence, otherwise state the actual status
For regression tests, verify with a red-green cycle: confirm the test passes, revert the fix and confirm it fails, then restore the fix
For delegated agents, verify their reported success against the actual version-control diff rather than trusting the report

skills.name: subagent-driven-development
skills.description: Use when executing implementation plans with independent tasks in the current session
skills.summary: Executes an implementation plan by dispatching a fresh subagent per task, followed by a two-stage spec-compliance then code-quality review before moving on.
skills.descriptionIntro: The skill runs a repeating per-task cycle of implementation, questions, and two-stage review.
descriptionBullets: Reads the full plan once, extracts every task's full text and context, and creates a TodoWrite entry per task before dispatching any subagent
Dispatches one implementer subagent per task, answering any questions it raises before letting it implement, test, commit, and self-review
Runs a mandatory two-stage review after each task: a spec-compliance reviewer first, then a code-quality reviewer, looping fixes back to the implementer until both approve
Selects model capability by task complexity: cheap models for mechanical single-file tasks, standard models for multi-file integration, and the most capable model for architecture or design judgment
Handles four implementer statuses (DONE, DONE_WITH_CONCERNS, NEEDS_CONTEXT, BLOCKED) with different escalation paths, never letting a blocked subagent silently retry unchanged
Dispatches a final code reviewer across the entire implementation after all tasks complete, then hands off to finishing-a-development-branch
howToUse: Read the plan once, extract every task's full text and context, and create a TodoWrite list covering all tasks
Dispatch a fresh implementer subagent for each task with its full text and context, then pauses to answer any clarifying questions before it proceeds
Let the implementer subagent implement, test, commit, and self-review the task
Dispatch a spec-compliance reviewer subagent, looping implementer fixes back through re-review until it approves
Dispatch a code-quality reviewer subagent only after spec compliance passes, looping fixes back through re-review until it approves
Mark the task complete and repeat for the remaining tasks
After all tasks are done, dispatch a final code reviewer for the entire implementation, then invoke finishing-a-development-branch

skills.name: enable-custom-dashboard-usage
skills.description: Ensures new or refactored Saporo frontend UI components are dashboard-ready by construction — self-sufficient via props with store defaults, scope passed as a prop rather than read from a global store, callback props instead of store writes/navigation, real API-backed data, and narrow-width safe layout — per the SPR-4648 guidelines, and optionally registers the component in the custom dashboard's WIDGET_REGISTRY so it's usable as a widget today. Use proactively whenever building or refactoring a non-trivial UI component/section/card, even if the user never says "widget" or "dashboard" — most page-level components are candidates for the custom dashboard system, and it's far cheaper to build them dashboard-ready than to retrofit later. Also trigger immediately whenever the user mentions a widget, dashboard, or reusable component.
skills.summary: Confirms with the user, then walks new/refactored UI components through a dashboard-readiness checklist and optionally registers them as widgets.
skills.descriptionIntro: Before registering a component as a dashboard widget, the skill works through a readiness checklist covering:
descriptionBullets: leaf/presentational components have zero store dependencies and fully typed props
section-level wrapper components accept props with store defaults as an escape hatch instead of hard store reads
no hard dependency on the global scopeSelection singleton — scope is accepted as a plain prop
callback props are used instead of direct store writes or imperative side effects like window.scrollTo
no dummy/fixture data — widgets must be backed by real API calls
no imperative route navigation — an onNavigate callback or plain link is used instead
layout stays safe at narrow dashboard grid widths
new widget categories are added consistently in both required locations
howToUse: Before doing anything, ask the user whether they want the component prepped for the custom dashboard system, since the trigger conditions deliberately over-fire, and skip entirely if they decline or the component is admin-only, preference-driven, or explicitly scoped to a single page.
Decide whether the component will be a self-fetching unwrapped widget or a thin wrapper around an existing section component.

Work through the readiness checklist, converting store reads to props with store defaults, replacing store writes/navigation with callback props, removing dummy data, and ensuring narrow-width layout safety.
Watch for red flags like deep structural coupling or page-shaped components that are a poor fit, and surface those to the user instead of forcing them through the checklist.

binding_property_non_reactive and ownership_invalid_binding binding errors
